topic: reflecting on final year — terrifying and exciting

turns.content: i just realised im like... actually nearly done with uni and i dont know how to feel about it
That's a big moment to sit with. Is it more exciting or terrifying right now, or a messy mix of both?

genuinely both at the same time which is a weird emotional place to be. like part of me is so ready to be done. no more assignments no more group projects no more scrambling to make deadlines at 3am. but also... uni has been my whole identity for like four years? i dont know who i am without a semester structure lol

The identity thing is real. When your entire routine, social life, and sense of purpose has been built around being a student for years, the idea of that framework just... ending is genuinely disorienting. Even people who are completely ready to leave often feel that weird vertigo.

What do you think you'll miss most?

honestly the people. like ive made some of my best friends through my degree and i know everyone says 'oh well stay in touch' but people get busy and scatter and its never quite the same as seeing someone in a tutorial every week. also ill miss the campus itself weirdly? like the library where ive had approximately nine thousand mental breakdowns has become a comfort space somehow. stockholm syndrome maybe

Developing emotional attachment to the place where you've suffered most is peak university experience. The library breakdowns become fond memories eventually — or at least funny stories.

And you're right that the social dynamic changes. It takes more effort to maintain friendships post-uni because there's no shared structure forcing you together. But the friends worth keeping are the ones where it doesn't feel like effort. Those people tend to stick around.

yeah i reckon my close ones will stick. its more the peripheral people — like the girl in my stats class who i always sat next to and complained about regression analysis with but never hung out with outside of class. those little connections that just... evaporate. idk why thats making me sad but it is

Those are called "weak ties" in sociology and they're actually a really meaningful part of daily life — the familiar faces, the routine interactions, the people you know just well enough to share a specific context with. Losing those all at once is a genuine loss even if each individual connection seems small.

It's okay to be sad about it. You're not just finishing a degree, you're leaving a whole ecosystem of people and places.

an ecosystem... the environmental science student in me appreciates that metaphor lol. but yeah thats exactly what it feels like. this whole interconnected system that ive been part of is about to just... change completely. and ill adapt because humans are adaptable or whatever but right now its lowkey terrifying

Ha, glad the metaphor landed. And yeah — transitions are terrifying even when they're ones you've been working toward. Finishing a degree is supposed to feel triumphant but nobody talks about the grief part of it.

Is there anything specific about what comes next that you're excited about? Sometimes it helps to balance the loss with the possibility.

i mean... yeah. im excited to have time? like actual free time that isnt stolen from something else. every weekend for the last four years has had this guilt cloud over it where im like 'you should be studying.' i want to know what its like to have a saturday where i just... exist. also im excited to apply everything ive learned to something real instead of hypothetical assignments

The guilt-free weekend thing is genuinely life-changing. That constant background hum of "I should be doing something" is exhausting and you don't fully realise how heavy it is until it's gone.

And wanting to apply your knowledge to real-world problems rather than academic exercises makes total sense — that's the whole point, right? You've spent four years building the toolkit. Now you get to actually use it.

yeah. yeah thats actually a nice way to think about it. ok i think im having a moment lol. its just wild that this chapter is ending. like i walked onto campus as an 18 year old who had no idea what she was doing and now im nearly done and i still dont fully know what im doing but at least i know more things now?? growth i guess

Knowing more things while still not fully knowing what you're doing is honestly the most accurate summary of what a degree gives you. Nobody figures it all out — you just get better at navigating the uncertainty.

You'll be fine. It's okay to feel all of this at once — the excitement, the terror, the nostalgia, the readiness. That just means you actually cared about the experience, which is more than a lot of people can say.

ok genuinely thank you for that because i think i needed to hear it. im gonna go be emotional about it for a bit and then probably make a coffee and stare at the wall contemplating the passage of time as one does. thanks for listening to my existential crisis lol
Coffee and wall-staring is a perfectly valid way to process an existential crisis. You've earned the moment. Good luck with the final stretch — the finish line is right there.
